%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.7-6 Matrix Operations

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to create, index, and manipulate matrices in MATLAB. You will master the syntax for building matrices from scratch, extracting submatrices using colon notation, solving linear systems efficiently, and using vectorization to compute many results at once instead of looping. These skills are essential for signal processing and scientific computing.

## Concepts In This Section

- common matrix generators
- manual matrix creation with brackets
- transpose and complex conjugate transpose
- matrix indexing and colon notation
- solving Ax = y with left division
- vectorization and outer products

## 1. Common Matrix Generators

MATLAB provides built-in functions to create standard matrices quickly. \(\text{eye}(m)\) creates an \(m \times m\) identity matrix, \(\text{ones}(m,n)\) creates an \(m \times n\) matrix of all ones, \(\text{zeros}(m,n)\) creates an \(m \times n\) matrix of all zeros, and \(\text{diag}(x)\) creates a diagonal matrix from vector \(x\). These functions save time and reduce errors when you need these common structures.

$$\text{eye}(3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{ones}(2,3) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{zeros}(2,2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
*These are the outputs of the three most common matrix generators. Notice that \(\text{eye}\) creates 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere, \(\text{ones}\) fills every entry with 1, and \(\text{zeros}\) fills every entry with 0.*

## 2. Creating Matrices by Hand

To create a matrix with arbitrary elements, use square brackets to enclose the entries. Separate elements in the same row with spaces or commas. Separate rows with semicolons. For example, \([1 \, 2 \, 3; 4 \, 5 \, 6]\) creates a \(2 \times 3\) matrix with first row \([1 \, 2 \, 3]\) and second row \([4 \, 5 \, 6]\). Each row must have the same number of elements, or MATLAB will report an error. This syntax is the foundation for building any matrix you need.

**Example:**
```
A = [2 3; 4 5; 0 6]
```
This produces a \(3 \times 2\) matrix.

## 3. Transpose and Complex Conjugate Transpose

In MATLAB, a single quote (apostrophe) performs the complex conjugate transpose. For real matrices, this is just the transpose: rows become columns and columns become rows. For complex matrices, the operation also takes the complex conjugate of each entry. The notation \(r'\) transposes vector \(r\). If \(r\) is a row vector, \(r'\) becomes a column vector, and vice versa. This is one of the most useful operations for reshaping data.

#### Note
Use \(r.\)' to perform transpose without conjugation if needed.

## 4. Matrix Indexing and Colon Notation

Matrix elements are accessed using two indices: (row, column). For example, \(B(1,2)\) retrieves the element in row 1, column 2. You can use vectors as indices to extract submatrices. The colon notation is especially powerful: \(B(1:2, 2:3)\) extracts rows 1–2 and columns 2–3. A single colon \(B(2,:)\) means all columns in row 2. This notation lets you slice and extract parts of matrices without loops, which is a key to efficient MATLAB code.

## 5. Solving Linear Systems

A common task is solving a system of linear equations \(Ax = y\), where \(A\) is a coefficient matrix, \(x\) is the unknown vector, and \(y\) is the right-hand side. In MATLAB, you can solve this by computing \(x = \text{inv}(A) \cdot y\) (using the matrix inverse) or more efficiently \(x = A \backslash y\) (using left division). The left-division operator is preferred because it is computationally more stable and faster, especially for large matrices. Both methods require that \(A\) be square and invertible. This approach is far faster than solving by hand or using Cramer's rule for systems with many equations.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.14](/figures/page-049-fig__b_14-1.png)
*Graphical comparison of two damped sinusoids with different decay rates.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">This figure shows two sinusoidal curves plotted over time \(t\) from 0 to 0.2 seconds. The solid curve \(f(t)\) oscillates with larger amplitude, while the dotted curve \(h(t)\) has smaller amplitude and decays faster. Both curves oscillate at the same frequency but with different damping. Students should notice how the two waveforms differ in peak height and decay rate, illustrating the effect of the damping parameter \(\alpha\) in the function \(h_\alpha(t) = e^{-\alpha t} \sin(2\pi f t + \varphi)\).</div>

## 6. Vectorization and the Outer-Product Trick

Vectorization is a technique that uses matrix operations to compute many results at once instead of using loops. For example, to compute a family of damped sinusoids \(h_\alpha(t) = e^{-\alpha t} \sin(2\pi \cdot 10 \cdot t + \pi/6)\) for \(\alpha = 0, 1, \ldots, 10\) and \(t\) from 0 to 0.2 seconds, you could write 11 separate loops. Instead, you can create a time matrix \(T\) by replicating the time vector using an outer product: \(T = t \cdot \text{ones}(1,11)\). This creates a \(201 \times 11\) matrix where each column is the same time vector. Then scale each column by the corresponding \(\alpha\) value using a diagonal matrix, and compute all 11 curves in one vectorized operation. This approach is much faster and produces cleaner, more readable code.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Create matrices with square brackets; separate row elements with spaces, rows with semicolons.
- Use colon notation \(A(i:j, k:l)\) to extract submatrices; \(A(:,n)\) means all rows of column \(n\).
- Solve linear systems \(Ax = y\) using \(x = A \backslash y\) (left division) for stability and efficiency.
- Vectorization replaces loops by using matrix operations; the outer product \(t \cdot \text{ones}(1,n)\) replicates vectors efficiently.

*In the next section we will explore more advanced MATLAB functions and signal processing applications.*
